items.title: Incidence Trends in Colorectal Cancer by Age and Demographics
items.content: The incidence of colorectal cancer is declining among adults over 50 due to widespread screening, but increasing in those under 50. This shift has led to higher mortality rates in younger populations.
keyPoints: 153,000 expected cases in 2024 (106,000 colon, 46,000 rectum)
53,000 expected deaths
Slight male predominance and more common in black Americans
Decline since 1985 due to screening guidelines
Increase in incidence under age 50
Leading cause of cancer death for young men (20–49) and second for women (40–49)
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.title: Adenomatous Polyps as a Premalignant Condition in Colorectal Cancer
items.content: Most colorectal cancers originate from adenomatous polyps, which are premalignant. Their risk of progressing to cancer is linked to size and histology.
keyPoints: Most colorectal cancers arise from adenomatous polyps
~25% of people have adenomatous polyps by age 50
Up to 50% of 70-year-old individuals may have adenomatous polyps
<5% progression rate to adenocarcinoma
Villous adenomas are three times more likely to develop into cancer than tubular adenomas
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Understanding Molecular Pathways in Sporadic Colorectal Cancer
items.content: Sporadic colorectal cancer involves three major molecular pathways: chromosomal instability (CIN), mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency, and CpG island methylation pathway (CIMP). The CIN pathway is the most prevalent and begins with loss of the APC gene. Subsequent mutations in oncogenes like KRAS and BRAF occur, followed by p53 loss. MMR defects often result from hypermethylation leading to MSI-high phenotype, which influences treatment sensitivity. CIMP tumors are frequently right-sided and may show MLH1 methylation.
keyPoints: CIN pathway is the most common in colon cancer, characterized by early APC gene loss.
Subsequent mutations involve KRAS and BRAF genes, with p53 loss as a final step.
MMR defects often cause MSI-high phenotype due to hypermethylation of repair gene promoters.
MSI-high tumors are sensitive to immune checkpoint inhibitors but less responsive to chemotherapy.
CIMP phenotype is associated with right-sided tumors and MLH1 methylation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Clinical Implications of Molecular Pathways in Colorectal Cancer Treatment
items.content: The molecular pathways in colorectal cancer have significant implications for treatment. Tumors with MSI-high due to MMR defects respond well to immune checkpoint inhibitors, unlike those treated with conventional chemotherapy. Understanding the pathway involvement helps tailor therapy, especially distinguishing between CIN and MSI-high tumors. The overlapping nature of these pathways means that individual cancers may exhibit characteristics from multiple pathways.
keyPoints: MSI-high tumors are sensitive to immune checkpoint inhibition but less so to chemotherapy.
CIN pathway involves APC gene loss followed by KRAS, BRAF mutations, and p53 loss.
MMR defects in Lynch syndrome involve germline mutations and result in MSI-high phenotype.
CIMP tumors can be either MSI-high or MSS and are often right-sided with MLH1 methylation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.title: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) and its Management
items.content: FAP is a hereditary condition caused by mutations in the APC gene, leading to hundreds to thousands of colonic polyps. It requires early intervention with colectomy to prevent colorectal cancer, which develops in nearly 100% of untreated individuals.
keyPoints: FAP accounts for <1% of all colon cancers.
Characterized by hundreds to thousands of polyps.
Colorectal cancer develops in virtually 100% if untreated.
Colectomy is recommended, especially when endoscopic screening is challenging.
NSAIDs may be used for chemoprevention due to their association with polyp regression.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: MUTYH-Associated Polyposis (MAP) and its Clinical Implications
items.content: MAP is an autosomal recessive condition caused by mutations in the MUTYH gene, leading to tens of polyps and increased risk for duodenal and thyroid cancers. Surveillance guidelines are less defined than FAP but generally recommend colonoscopic surveillance starting at age 25-30.
keyPoints: MAP accounts for <1% of colorectal cancers.
Caused by biallelic mutations in the MUTYH gene.
Increased risk for duodenal and thyroid cancers.
Colonoscopic surveillance recommended annually to biennially starting at age 25-30.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Lynch Syndrome: Identification and Implications
items.content: Lynch syndrome, the most common inherited colorectal cancer predisposition, is due to mutations in MMR genes. It confers a significant risk for colorectal and various extracolonic cancers. Universal testing of tumors for MMR defects is recommended.
keyPoints: Accounts for ~3% of all colorectal cancers.
Confers up to 17-fold increased risk of colorectal cancer.
Caused by mutations in MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 genes or EPCAM deletion.
Associated with multiple extracolonic cancers.
Universal testing for MMR defects recommended; family history aids identification.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.title: Dietary Influence on Colorectal Cancer Risk
items.content: The risk of colorectal cancer is significantly influenced by dietary factors, particularly the consumption of animal fats and calories. High intake of red meats and processed meats increases gut anaerobes, leading to carcinogenic bile acid conversion. Additionally, Western diets high in calories contribute to obesity and insulin resistance, further elevating cancer risk.
keyPoints: Colorectal cancer incidence is linked to higher consumption of animal fats, meat protein, dietary fat/oil, and calories.
Animal fats increase gut anaerobes like Fusobacterium nucleatum and Bacteroides fragilis, converting bile acids into carcinogens.
Western diets high in calories lead to obesity and insulin resistance, increasing circulating IGF-I which stimulates intestinal mucosa proliferation.
Diet is a major etiologic factor for colorectal cancer, especially those high in animal fat and calories.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.title: Lack of Protective Effect from Dietary Fiber
items.content: Contrary to previous beliefs, dietary fiber and diets rich in fruits and vegetables do not prevent colorectal adenomas or cancer. Despite this, diet remains a major factor in colorectal cancer etiology, with high animal fat and calorie intake being particularly significant.
keyPoints: Randomized trials and case-controlled studies show no preventive effect of dietary fiber or fruit/vegetable-rich diets on colorectal adenomas or cancer.
Dietary habits significantly influence colorectal cancer risk, especially those involving high animal fat and caloric intake.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.title: Increased Risk of Colorectal Cancer in IBD Patients
items.content: Patients with inflammatory bowel disease, particularly those with ulcerative colitis, have an increased risk of developing colorectal cancer over time. The risk is relatively small during the first 10 years but increases at a rate of approximately 0.5-1% per year thereafter.
keyPoints: Risk of colorectal cancer in IBD patients increases after 10 years of disease onset.
Annual increase in risk: ~0.5-1% per year post the first decade.
Cancer incidence in IBD patients ranges from 8-30%.
Higher risk associated with more extensive, severe, and longer-lasting colitis.
Molecular pathogenesis differs from sporadic cancers; K-ras and APC mutations are less common.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.title: Surveillance Strategies for Colorectal Cancer in IBD Patients
items.content: Due to the lack of a classic polyp-to-cancer sequence, surveillance strategies for colorectal cancer in IBD patients are complex and require specialized management. Surgical removal of the colon can significantly reduce or eliminate cancer risk.
keyPoints: Surveillance is complicated by similar symptoms between tumor presence and disease flare-ups.
Management requires specialists due to technical complexity.
Surgical removal (subtotal or total colectomy) reduces colorectal cancer risk in ulcerative colitis patients.
Post-subtotal colectomy, surveillance of the remaining rectum is necessary.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.title: Aspirin Use in Colon Cancer Prevention
items.content: Regular aspirin use has been shown to reduce the risk of colon adenomas and potentially decrease the incidence of colon cancer, though results from prospective trials are conflicting. The preventive effects may increase with longer duration and higher dosage of aspirin but can take years to manifest.
keyPoints: Aspirin suppresses cell proliferation by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis.
Randomized studies show reduced risk of colon adenomas with regular aspirin use.
Cohort studies demonstrate a reduced incidence of colon cancer with regular aspirin use.
Meta-analyses suggest that regular aspirin use prevents colon cancer.
Preventive effects may increase with longer duration and higher dosage of aspirin but can take years to manifest.
Regular aspirin use is an individualized decision due to potential side effects.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.title: Ineffectiveness of Antioxidant Vitamins in Adenoma Prevention
items.content: Antioxidant vitamins such as ascorbic acid, tocopherols, and $ \beta $-carotene do not reduce the incidence of subsequent adenomas after removal of a colon adenoma. Estrogen replacement therapy may lower colorectal cancer incidence in women but does not affect mortality.
keyPoints: Antioxidant vitamins are ineffective at reducing subsequent adenomas post-removal.
Estrogen replacement therapy reduces colorectal cancer incidence in women but not mortality.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.title: Why Lower the Age for Colorectal Cancer Screening?
items.content: The U.S. Preventative Services Task Force lowered the recommended age for colorectal cancer screening to 45 due to a rise in incidence among younger adults.
keyPoints: Screening recommendations updated in 2021
Lowered age threshold from previous guidelines to 45
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.title: Limitations of Fecal Occult Blood Testing
items.content: Fecal occult blood testing has limitations as a screening tool, with about 50% of colorectal cancer patients having negative results due to intermittent bleeding patterns.
keyPoints: Approximately 50% of colorectal cancers have negative fecal occult blood tests
2–4% of asymptomatic persons test positive for fecal occult blood
<10% of 'test-positive' cases are colorectal cancers
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.title: Efficacy and Limitations of Multitarget Stool DNA Testing
items.content: Multitarget stool DNA testing has high specificity (>90%) and sensitivity (>90%) for detecting colorectal adenocarcinoma, but lower sensitivity (<50%) for advanced precancerous lesions.
keyPoints: >90% specificity and sensitivity for colorectal adenocarcinoma
<50% sensitivity for advanced precancerous lesions
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.title: Virtual Colonoscopy: An Alternative to Endoscopic Techniques
items.content: Virtual colonoscopy, developed with the advent of CT scanning, offers an alternative screening method for colorectal cancer. It requires bowel preparation and is effective at detecting large lesions (>10 mm). However, it lacks randomized comparison with traditional colonoscopy and may require follow-up colonoscopy if findings are present.
keyPoints: Virtual colonoscopy detects large lesions (>10 mm) effectively.
Requires colon cathartic bowel preparations.
Lacks randomized study comparison with colonoscopy.
Findings necessitate follow-up with traditional colonoscopy, requiring a second bowel preparation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.title: Initiating Colon Cancer Screening at Age 45
items.content: Most professional societies recommend starting colorectal cancer screening at age 45 for average-risk individuals. In the U.S., compliance is around 60%, with initial screenings using sigmoidoscopy, colonoscopy, FIT, or multitarget stool DNA tests.
keyPoints: Screening should begin at age 45 for average-risk patients.
~60% compliance rate in the United States.
Initial screening methods include sigmoidoscopy, colonoscopy, FIT, and multitarget stool DNA.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.title: Iron-Deficiency Anemia as a Red Flag for Right Colon Cancer
items.content: Patients with tumors in the ascending colon often present with hypochromic, microcytic anemia due to chronic blood loss. This symptom necessitates thorough endoscopic and/or radiographic examination of the large bowel.
keyPoints: Iron-deficiency anemia is a red flag for right colon cancer.
Unexplained iron-deficiency anemia in adults (except premenopausal, multiparous women) requires full large bowel visualization.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Characteristic Imaging Findings of Colorectal Tumors
items.content: Tumors in the transverse and descending colon often cause obstructive symptoms, with imaging revealing 'apple-core' lesions. Rectosigmoid tumors may present with hematochezia and tenesmus.
keyPoints: 'Apple-core' lesions on radiographs/CTs indicate constricting tumors in the transverse or descending colon.
Rectal bleeding and altered bowel habits necessitate digital rectal examination and sigmoidoscopy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Staging and Prognostic Factors in Colorectal Cancer
items.content: Colorectal cancer prognosis is determined by TNM classification, with stages I-IV based on tumor penetration, lymph node involvement, and metastases. Improved survival rates are linked to better detection of metastatic disease and chemotherapy.
keyPoints: TNM staging: Stage I (T1-2N0M0), II (T3-4N0M0), III (TXN1-2M0), IV (TXNXM1).
5-year survival is stage-related, with better outcomes due to improved metastatic detection and chemotherapy.
At least 12 lymph nodes should be sampled for accurate staging.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Management of MSI-High Rectal Cancers with Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.content: For patients diagnosed with microsatellite instability-high (MSI-high) rectal cancers, checkpoint inhibitors such as PD-1 antibodies can be used as a curative treatment without the need for surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1 antibodies) can treat MSI-high rectal cancers without surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy.
Studies are ongoing for managing MSI-high colon cancers with checkpoint inhibition.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.title: Adjuvant Chemotherapy in MSS Colon Cancer
items.content: For patients with high-risk stage II and stage III microsatellite stable (MSS) colon adenocarcinoma, adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended to improve overall survival and cure rates by approximately 30%. The standard treatment involves either a single-agent fluoropyrimidine or in combination with oxaliplatin for 3–6 months.
keyPoints: Adjuvant chemotherapy improves overall survival and cure rates by ~30% in high-risk stage II and III MSS colon cancer.
Standard adjuvant treatments include single-agent fluoropyrimidine (IV or oral) or combined with oxaliplatin for 3–6 months.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.title: Standard Curative Therapy for MSS Rectal Adenocarcinoma
items.content: Surgical resection is the standard curative therapy for most patients with microsatellite stable (MSS) rectal adenocarcinoma. Pelvic MRI should be used to assess mesorectal margin and nodal status in all patients.
keyPoints: Surgical resection is considered the standard curative therapy for MSS rectal adenocarcinoma.
Pelvic MRI is recommended for staging to evaluate mesorectal margin and nodal status.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.title: Preoperative Therapy Alternatives and Nonoperative Management in Rectal Cancer
items.content: For patients with stage II and III rectal cancer, preoperative therapy can reduce the risk of local recurrence. In cases where radiation is unacceptable or if a complete clinical response occurs after total preoperative therapy, nonoperative management may be considered.
keyPoints: Preoperative chemotherapy and radiation can reduce the risk of local recurrence in stage II and III rectal cancer.
For patients with unacceptable risks from radiation (e.g., infertility), fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin followed by surgery is an alternative.
60–70% of patients with a complete clinical response after total preoperative therapy can be cured without surgical resection.
30–40% of patients may experience recurrence, necessitating intensive follow-up.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.title: Curative Potential of Complete Resection in Isolated Metastases
items.content: Complete resection of metastatic colorectal cancer is considered curative, especially when metastases are isolated to a single site such as the liver, lungs, or peritoneum. Surgical consultation is advised before initiating systemic chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Complete resection can be curative for isolated metastases (liver, lungs, peritoneum).
Consult surgical specialists before starting systemic chemotherapy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.title: Treatment Approach Based on MSI Status and RAS/RAF Mutations
items.content: Patients with MSI-high colon cancer should receive checkpoint inhibitors as initial treatment. For MSS colorectal adenocarcinoma, systemic chemotherapy improves survival compared to palliative care alone. Molecular genotyping is crucial; patients without RAS/RAF mutations benefit from EGF antibodies like cetuximab and panitumumab.
keyPoints: MSI-high colon cancer treated with checkpoint inhibitors.
Systemic chemotherapy extends life by 2–3 years, with up to 25% living ≥5 years.
Molecular genotyping identifies RAS/RAF status; non-mutated patients respond well to EGF antibodies.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.title: Therapeutic Options and Considerations for Specific Mutations
items.content: Patients with BRAF V600E mutations may require targeted therapy such as encorafenib combined with cetuximab. HER2 overexpression warrants anti-HER2-targeted therapies like tucatinib and trastuzumab, especially in RAS mutation-negative cases.
keyPoints: BRAF V600E mutations indicate a need for encorafenib plus cetuximab.
HER2-positive colorectal cancers benefit from tucatinib and trastuzumab if RAS-negative.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.question: What is the most common type of polyp from which colorectal cancers arise?
items.explanation: Most colorectal cancers arise from adenomatous polyps.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.question: What percentage of people in the United States have adenomatous polyps by age 50?
items.explanation: Approximately 25% of people have adenomatous polyps by age 50.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.question: Which type of adenoma is more likely to develop into cancer?
items.explanation: Villous adenomas develop into cancer threefold more often than tubular adenomas.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: Which molecular pathway is most commonly associated with colon cancer?
items.explanation: The chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway is the most common molecular pathway associated with colon cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: What characterizes tumors with a defect in the mismatch repair (MMR) pathway?
items.explanation: Defects in the MMR pathway result in microsatellite instability high (MSI-high) characterized by altered sizes of repeat sequences.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: Which gene is often lost early in the chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway?
items.explanation: The CIN pathway is characterized by an early loss of the adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) gene.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP)?
items.explanation: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis is due to a mutation in the APC gene.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.question: What pattern of inheritance does Lynch syndrome follow?
items.explanation: Lynch syndrome is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of colorectal cancers are accounted for by Lynch syndrome?
items.explanation: Lynch syndrome accounts for ~3% of all colorectal cancers.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.question: Which gene is involved in MUTYH-Associated Polyposis?
items.explanation: MUTYH-Associated Polyposis is caused by a biallelic mutation in the MUTYH gene.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.question: Which dietary component is associated with an increased proportion of anaerobes in the gut microflora, potentially leading to colorectal cancer?
items.explanation: Ingestion of animal fats is linked to increased anaerobes in the gut, which may convert bile acids into carcinogens.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.question: What condition associated with Western diets contributes to colorectal cancer risk through elevated levels of insulin-like growth factor type I (IGF-I)?
items.explanation: Obesity and insulin resistance from high-calorie diets lead to increased IGF-I, which may stimulate intestinal mucosa proliferation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.question: Which dietary factor has failed to show any value in preventing the recurrence of colorectal adenomas or development of colorectal cancer according to randomized trials and case-controlled studies?
items.explanation: Studies have not shown dietary fiber or high fruit/vegetable diets to prevent colorectal adenomas or cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.question: Which gene mutations are less common in colon cancers arising from inflammatory bowel disease compared to sporadic colorectal cancers?
items.explanation: K-ras and APC mutations are less common in colon cancers arising from IBD patients.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.question: In which type of inflammatory bowel disease is the risk of colorectal cancer more consistently documented?
options: Both Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis equally
items.explanation: Most data supporting the increased risk of colorectal cancer in IBD come from patients with ulcerative colitis.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.question: What is a major challenge in the surveillance for colorectal cancer in patients with inflammatory bowel disease?
options: The classic polyp-to-cancer sequence is present.
Symptoms of tumor appearance are distinct from IBD flare-ups.
Surveillance requires specialized management due to symptom overlap.
Cancer risk decreases significantly after 10 years.
items.explanation: The lack of the classic polyp-to-cancer sequence and similar symptoms make surveillance challenging in IBD patients.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.question: Which class of chemopreventive agents has been shown to reduce the risk of colon adenomas?
items.explanation: Aspirin and other NSAIDs have been shown to reduce the risk of colon adenomas by suppressing cell proliferation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.question: What is the effect of regular aspirin use on the incidence of colorectal cancer according to cohort studies?
items.explanation: Cohort studies have shown that regular aspirin use reduces the incidence of colorectal cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.question: Which statement is true regarding antioxidant vitamins and adenomas?
options: They effectively reduce the incidence of subsequent adenomas.
They are ineffective at reducing the incidence of subsequent adenomas.
They increase the risk of developing adenomas.
Their effect on adenomas varies significantly between individuals.
items.explanation: Antioxidant vitamins such as ascorbic acid, tocopherols, and $  eta $-carotene are ineffective at reducing the incidence of subsequent adenomas in patients who have undergone the removal of a colon adenoma.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.question: Which screening test has been shown to have a major limitation due to the intermittent bleeding pattern of colorectal tumors?
items.explanation: The fecal occult blood test has major limitations as a screening technique due to the intermittent bleeding pattern of colorectal tumors.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.question: What is the sensitivity of the multitarget stool DNA test for detecting an advanced precancerous lesion?
items.explanation: The multitarget stool DNA test has a <50% sensitivity for detecting an advanced precancerous lesion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.question: Which screening strategy is associated with less complications and does not require sedation?
items.explanation: Sigmoidoscopy is associated with less complications and does not require sedation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.question: What change did the U.S. Preventative Services Task Force make to colorectal cancer screening recommendations in 2021?
options: Increased the age for recommended screening to 55
Lowered the age for recommended screening to 45
Recommended annual colonoscopy for all adults over 50
items.explanation: The U.S. Preventative Services Task Force lowered the age for recommended screening to 45.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.question: What is a limitation of virtual colonography compared to endoscopic screening techniques?
options: It has been proven superior in randomized studies.
Findings need follow-up with a colonoscopy requiring another bowel cathartic preparation.
items.explanation: Virtual colonography findings require follow-up with a colonoscopy, necessitating a second bowel preparation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.question: Which imaging study was used prior to fiberoptic endoscopy to identify sources of occult blood in the stool?
items.explanation: Air contrast barium enemas were used before fiberoptic endoscopy to identify sources of occult blood.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended starting age for colorectal cancer screening in patients at average risk according to most professional societies?
items.explanation: Most professional societies recommend beginning colorectal cancer screening at age 45 for patients at average risk.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.question: Which symptom is most commonly associated with tumors in the ascending colon?
options: Chronic, insidious blood loss leading to hypochromic, microcytic anemia
Narrowing of the caliber of stool
items.explanation: Tumors in the ascending colon often lead to chronic blood loss without a change in stool appearance, resulting in iron-deficiency anemia.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a characteristic radiographic finding for tumors in the transverse and descending colon?
items.explanation: Tumors in the transverse and descending colon often show 'apple-core' lesions on radiographs.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a common presenting symptom for rectosigmoid tumors?
options: Hematochezia, tenesmus, and narrowing of stool caliber
Chronic blood loss without change in stool appearance
items.explanation: Rectosigmoid tumors often present with hematochezia, tenesmus, and narrowed stool.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is the most frequent visceral site of metastasis for colorectal cancer?
items.explanation: The liver is the most common site of metastasis in colorectal cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which of the following is considered the standard initial treatment for patients with MSI-high rectal cancer?
options: Surgery with adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation
Checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1 antibodies) without surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy
Initial surgical resection followed by observation
items.explanation: MSI-high rectal cancers can be treated for cure with checkpoint inhibitors without the need for surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.question: What is the standard adjuvant treatment approach for patients with high-risk stage II and III MSS colon cancer?
options: Adjuvant chemotherapy for 3–6 months, either with a single-agent fluoropyrimidine or in combination with oxaliplatin
Immediate surgery followed by radiation therapy
Checkpoint inhibitors as the sole adjuvant treatment
items.explanation: Adjuvant chemotherapy for 3–6 months either with a single-agent fluoropyrimidine or in combination with oxaliplatin is considered standard.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.question: Following complete surgical resection of stage I colon cancer, what is the expected outcome for patients?
options: They are considered cured and require no further treatment.
They need immediate adjuvant chemotherapy to prevent recurrence.
Regular radiation therapy is necessary post-surgery.
They should undergo surgery again within a year.
items.explanation: Patients with stage I disease are considered cured following complete surgical resection.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.question: Which surgical procedure for localized rectal cancer necessitates a permanent colostomy?
items.explanation: An abdominal-perineal resection (APR) requires a permanent colostomy due to the resection of the anal sphincter.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.question: What imaging modality is recommended for staging rectal cancer to assess mesorectal margin and nodal status?
items.explanation: Pelvic MRI should be standard in staging for all patients with rectal cancer to assess mesorectal margin and nodal status.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.question: Which treatment option is considered when radiation therapy's long-term side effects are deemed unacceptable?
options: Preoperative therapy with a fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin followed by surgical resection
items.explanation: Preoperative therapy with a fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin followed by surgical resection can be considered for patients when radiation therapy's long-term side effects are deemed unacceptable.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.question: Which of the following treatments is recommended as initial therapy for MSI-high colon cancer?
items.explanation: Patients with MSI-high colon cancer should be treated with a checkpoint inhibitor as initial treatment.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.question: What is the average survival time for patients with metastatic colorectal cancer receiving systemic chemotherapy?
items.explanation: Patients with metastatic disease live on average for 2–3 years with systemic chemotherapy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.question: Which medication is associated with the risk of permanent peripheral neuropathy?
items.explanation: Oxaliplatin is a platinum analogue that can cause permanent peripheral neuropathy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.question: Which genetic mutation limits the effectiveness of cetuximab and panitumumab in right-sided colon cancers?
items.explanation: Right-sided colon cancers have upregulation of receptor tyrosine kinases that create resistance to cetuximab and panitumumab.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.statement: Adenomatous polyps are always symptomatic and bleed.
items.explanation: The vast majority of adenomatous polyps are asymptomatic and do not bleed.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.statement: There has been a decline in the incidence of colorectal cancer in adults older than 50 since 1985.
items.explanation: Since 1985, there has been a steady decline in the incidence of colorectal cancer in both men and women over 50 due to widespread screening guidelines.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.statement: The CIMP phenotype is always associated with microsatellite instability high (MSI-high).
items.explanation: CIMP tumors can be either MSI-high or microsatellite stable (MSS).
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.statement: Loss of the p53 gene is a common final step in the chromosomal instability pathway.
items.explanation: The loss of the p53 tumor suppressor gene is indeed a common final step in the CIN pathway.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.statement: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) is characterized by hundreds to thousands of colonic polyps.
items.explanation: FAP is indeed characterized by the development of hundreds to thousands of colonic polyps.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.statement: BRCA1 mutation confers a 2-fold increased risk of colorectal cancer.
items.explanation: BRCA1 mutation may confer a 1.5-fold increased risk, not 2-fold.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.statement: Geographic variations in the incidence of large-bowel cancer are primarily due to genetic differences.
items.explanation: Migrant groups adopt the colorectal cancer rates of their new countries, indicating environmental rather than genetic factors.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.statement: Dietary fiber has been proven to prevent the development of colorectal cancer.
items.explanation: Randomized trials and studies have failed to show any preventive value for dietary fiber in colorectal cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.statement: Cigarette smoking is linked to the development of colorectal adenomas after more than 35 years of tobacco use.
items.explanation: Tobacco use, especially with >35 years of smoking, is associated with the development of colorectal adenomas.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS
items.statement: Vitamin D supplementation has been proven to reduce the incidence of colon cancer.
items.explanation: No evidence suggests that vitamin D supplementation results in fewer colon cancers.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE (CHAP. 337) | OTHER HIGH-RISK CONDITIONS

items.statement: Estrogen replacement therapy reduces mortality from colorectal cancer.
items.explanation: Estrogen replacement therapy is associated with a reduction in the incidence of colorectal cancer but not mortality.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.statement: Meta-analyses suggest that regular aspirin use prevents colon cancer.
items.explanation: Meta-analyses have suggested that regular aspirin use prevents colon cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.statement: A colorectal neoplasm will be found in most asymptomatic individuals with occult blood in their stool.
items.explanation: Most asymptomatic individuals with occult blood in their stool do not have a colorectal neoplasm.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.statement: The benefit of annual stool guaiac screening emerged after many years due to colonoscopic intervention.
items.explanation: The reduction in mortality rate from colorectal cancer with annual stool guaiac screening only became evident after many years, likely due to colonoscopic interventions.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.statement: Virtual colonography has been compared in a randomized study with colonoscopy.
items.explanation: Virtual colonography has not been compared to colonoscopy in a randomized study.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.statement: The compliance rate with colorectal cancer screening in the United States is approximately 60%.
items.explanation: There is ~60% compliance with colorectal cancer screening in the United States.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.statement: Colorectal cancer prognosis is influenced by the size of the primary lesion.
items.explanation: Prognosis is not influenced by tumor size when adjusted for nodal involvement and histologic differentiation.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: The majority of colorectal cancer recurrences occur within the first 5 years after resection.
items.explanation: Most recurrences happen within the initial 5 years post-resection.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Anastomotic recurrences are common in colorectal cancer patients if the surgical margins were adequate and free of tumor.
items.explanation: Anastomotic recurrences are infrequent provided the surgical resection margins were adequate and free of tumor.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.statement: Patients who have been cured of one colorectal cancer have a 3–5% probability of developing an additional bowel cancer during their lifetime.
items.explanation: There is a 3–5% probability of developing another bowel cancer in patients who have been cured.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | LOCALIZED COLON CANCER

items.statement: Radiation therapy is associated with a risk of malignancy approximately 1% after 10 years.
items.explanation: Radiation therapy has a ~1% risk of causing malignancy after 10 years.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.statement: Patients with stage II and III rectal cancer are not considered for preoperative therapy.
items.explanation: Stage II and III rectal cancer patients have a high enough risk to warrant consideration of preoperative therapy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.statement: Resection of the primary tumor in patients with unresectable metastatic disease improves overall survival.
items.explanation: Studies have demonstrated that resection of the primary tumor in the setting of unresectable metastatic disease does not improve overall survival.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.statement: Patients with BRAF V600E mutations often respond well to standard chemotherapy.
items.explanation: BRAF V600E mutations are associated with a particularly virulent form of colon cancer that does not respond as well to standard chemotherapy.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.assertion: The risk of progression from adenomatous polyps to cancer is associated with the size and histology of the polyp.
items.reason: Villous adenomas develop into cancer threefold more often than tubular adenomas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains that villous adenomas have a higher risk of progression to cancer.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · Colorectal Cancer | INCIDENCE | ADENOMATOUS POLYPS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.assertion: Defects in the MMR pathway are associated with Lynch syndrome.
items.reason: Lynch syndrome involves a germline mutation in one of the genes encoding for proteins involved in the MMR pathway.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS | ETIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.assertion: Lynch syndrome is the most common inherited predisposition to colorectal cancer.
items.reason: It accounts for ~3% of all colorectal cancers and confers an up to 17-fold risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Lynch syndrome is common.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with MUTYH-Associated Polyposis should undergo colectomy.
items.reason: MAP accounts for <1% of colorectal cancers and has clear screening guidelines.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as MAP's screening guidelines are less clear.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 1)

items.assertion: The incidence of colorectal cancer has increased in Japan since adopting a more Western diet.
items.reason: A Western diet is associated with higher consumption of animal fats and calories, which are linked to an increased risk of colorectal cancer.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why Japan's shift to a Western diet correlates with increased colorectal cancer incidence.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · HEREDITARY FACTORS AND SYNDROMES (part 2)

items.assertion: Aspirin has a delayed effect on preventing colorectal cancer.
items.reason: Prevention may increase with the duration and dosage of aspirin, and effects may take years to manifest.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why aspirin's prevention effect on colorectal cancer is delayed.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · PRIMARY PREVENTION | SCREENING

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.assertion: Flexible sigmoidoscopy is recommended over colonoscopy in some screening strategies.
items.reason: It requires no sedation, has fewer complications, and can identify patients at high risk of needing a full colonoscopy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)
items.assertion: The fecal occult blood test has a high sensitivity for detecting colorectal cancers.
items.reason: About 50% of patients with documented colorectal cancers have a negative fecal occult blood test.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the fecal occult blood test does not have high sensitivity, as evidenced by the fact that about 50% of patients with documented colorectal cancers have a negative result.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)
items.assertion: Virtual colonography has good accuracy at detecting large lesions (>10 mm).
items.reason: Virtual colonography requires use of colon cathartic bowel preparations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · TABLE 86-3 Screening Strategies for Colorectal Cancer (part 2)

items.assertion: Tumors with microsatellite instability have an improved prognosis.
items.reason: This is due to an enhanced immune response against the tumor.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Tumors in the right colon have a better prognosis than those in the left.
items.reason: This is due to differences in molecular patterns between tumors in different parts of the colon.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; tumors in the left colon have a better prognosis.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Nonoperative management can be considered for patients with stage II and III rectal cancer who experience a complete clinical response.
items.reason: 60–70% of these patients will be cured without an LAR or APR.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why nonoperative management can be considered.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · LOCALIZED RECTAL CANCER

items.assertion: Molecular genotyping should be done in all patients with metastatic colon cancer.
items.reason: Patients without mutations of the RAS/RAF pathway can have excellent responses and prolonged survival with EGF antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER

items.assertion: Untreated hypertension from bevacizumab can lead to posterior reversible leukoencephalopathy syndrome.
items.reason: Bevacizumab is a monoclonal antibody against vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Colorectal Cancer · METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER
